attributes.stem:
A 70-year old male presents to his GP after noticing a neck swelling. He first noticed it 2 years ago and it has slowly become larger since then. He reveals no symptoms of hypo or hyper thyroidism. He has recently noticed trouble breathing and facial flushing when he lays down, which prompted his presentation.

On examination his vital signs are within normal range. He has no nail changes or tremor. He has a thyroid mass that is palpable, it has many multi-nodular components to it and the lower pole is not palpable with his neck hyper-extended. Percussion across his chest is dull and when he is asked to raise his hands above his head the following sign is observed.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's appearance?
attributes.explanation:
The most common cause of substernal/restrosternal goitres is a multi-nodular goitre that has grown slowly over many years. These types of goitres are large and can produce symptoms such as problems breathing, wheezing, cough, hoarse voice, stridor or dysphagia. They can also cause a positive Pemberton’s sign where when the patient is asked to raise their hands high over their head the goitre obstructs the thoracic inlet and produces a transient superior vena caval syndrome. This is represented with the triad of trouble breathing, stridor and facial flushing. This sign was discovered by Hugh Pemberton after he noticed symptoms that developed from these goitres due to changes in position such as lying flat, raising their arms or with neck movements. A CT scan should be ordered to show how far it extends and to guide management, which most often involves surgery.

Trousseau’s sign is when there is flexion of the wrist and thumb with hyperextension of the fingers (carpopedal spasm) induced after inflation of the blood pressure cuff. The cuff often needs to be inflated to 20mmHg over the systolic blood pressure for 3 minutes to elicit. It can indicate hypocalcaemia.

Bancroft’s sign (also called Moses’ sign) is positive when a patient gets pain from the calf muscle being pushed forwards against the tibia but not when it is pushed side to side. It is a suggestive of a deep vein thrombosis of the lower leg.

Carnett’s sign refers to whether acute abdominal pain changes based on contraction of the abdominal muscles. If tenderness is unchanged or increased with abdominal wall contraction it suggests that the pain may be from here rather than an intra-abdominal pathology.

Froment’s sign is when a patient struggles to or is unable to make and maintain a pinch grip between the thumb and index finger. It can be demonstrated by getting the patient to pinch a piece of paper and pulling the paper away. It suggests ulnar nerve palsy as the adductor pollicis and interossei muscles are weak and there is compensatory flexing of the tip of the thumb due to the flexor pollicis longus muscle.
learningPoints: Pemberton's sign is positive when a patient lifts their arms above their head and they demonstrate trouble breathing, facial flushing and stridor
Pemberton's sign suggests there is a mass obstructing the thoracic inlet (often a large restrosternal goitre)
references: Patel M, Hu EW. Trousseau Sign. [Updated 2020 Jul 18]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2021 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK557832/
Merli G, Weitz H 2016. The consult guys – Homans sign: A sign of what? American College of Physicians. Access at: https://doi.org/10.7326/W16-0003
Suleiman S, Johnston DE. The abdominal wall: an overlooked source of pain. Am Fam Physician. 2001 Aug 1;64(3):431-8. PMID: 11515832.
MedGen UID: 713325. Froment sign. Access at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/medgen/713325#Definition.

Aghajanzadeh M, Asgary MR, Mohammadi F, Darvishi H, Safarpour Y. An investigation into symptoms, diagnosis, treatment, and treatment complications in patients with retrosternal goiter. J Family Med Prim Care. 2018;7(1):224-229. doi:10.4103/jfmpc.jfmpc_286_17
Clayman G. Substernal Goiter: Diagnosis and Treatment of Substeernal Goitre. Clayman Thyroid Centre. Access at: https://www.thyroidcancer.com/goiters/substernal-goiters.
